_status: HARVESTED 2026-06-17 — DFPT el-ph DONE on vast pod 41069486 (WRAPPER_EXIT=0, run_elph.sh DONE, PHONON WALL 22h59m). VERDICT: dynamically UNSTABLE at 2x2x2 q -> lambda/Tc UNTRUSTWORTHY as absolute (d6 gate).
structure: CeCo3B2-type Ru-kagome, hexagonal P6/mmm (No.191), ibrav=4, nat=6 ntyp=3 (La 1a triangular z=0; Ru 3g KAGOME z=1/2; Si 2c honeycomb z=0).
method: QE 7.5 DFPT (ph.x) electron_phonon='simple' double-delta a2F; PBE NC SG15 ONCV (La z11/Ru z16/Si z4, 67 e-); ecutwfc=90 ecutrho=360 Ry; MP smearing degauss=0.02 Ry; nspin=1 (NM ground state confirmed by gate, m=0).
qmesh: 2x2x2 (ldisp, Gamma-centered irreducible set, 4 irreducible q; star multiplicities w=[1,1,3,3], total=8)
smearing_used: el_ph_sigma index 10 = 0.050 Ry Gaussian (the broadening at which DOS=35.4078 the task reference; el_ph_nsigma=10)

stability.detail: imaginary (negative-omega) modes appear at EVERY q, NOT just the 3 acoustic-at-Gamma artifacts. dyn1(Gamma): 3 modes (-29.7,-29.7,-21.0 cm-1) = acoustic-Gamma ASR artifacts (borderline, explainable). dyn2(q=0,0,-0.800): 5 modes incl GENUINE soft -77.1,-70.2,-70.2 cm-1. dyn3(q=0,-0.577,0): 2 GENUINE -44.2,-35.5 cm-1. dyn4(q=0,-0.577,-0.800): 1 GENUINE -61.5 cm-1. The 6 large non-Gamma negatives are real soft modes, not numerics.
stability.note_on_lambda_neg: ph.x prints per-mode lambda(nu)<0 for every imaginary mode (e.g. lambda(1)=-0.1241 at dyn4 = the -61.5 cm-1 soft mode; NOT an acoustic-Gamma artifact, q4 != Gamma). The negative per-mode lambda is the direct signature of imaginary omega^2 in lambda_qnu = gamma/(pi N(Ef) omega^2).

lambda_tc_interpretation: TWO assemblies. (1) NAIVE (lambda.x convention = sum ALL modes incl negative-lambda soft modes): lambda=-0.339 (unphysical; imaginary modes contribute -1.98), Tc=0. This is what lambda.x reports and is why an unstable lattice gives a meaningless Tc. (2) PHYSICAL (imaginary modes dropped, only the 7 stable q-modes carrying real el-ph): lambda=1.64, omega_log=56.3 K, Tc(0.10)=6.96 K, Tc(0.13)=6.40 K -- strikingly matches measured 7 K BUT requires discarding 11 modes so it is a soft UPPER BOUND, not a converged value.
validation_verdict: 🔴 DYNAMICALLY UNSTABLE at 2x2x2 -> absolute lambda/Tc UNTRUSTWORTHY (d6). The 6 large non-Gamma soft modes (down to -77 cm-1) corrupt the canonical (lambda.x) sum to lambda=-0.34/Tc=0. The physical-only sum (imaginary dropped) gives lambda=1.64 -> Tc~7K matching the real material, suggesting the el-ph PHYSICS is right but the 2x2x2 q-grid is too COARSE to stabilize the kagome lattice (soft modes = likely grid-aliasing / under-convergence rather than a true CDW, common for kagome metals on coarse q). REQUIRES 4x4x4 q to decide: do the soft modes harden (artifact, then trust lambda~1.6) or persist (real instability)?
imaginary_modes: 11 total across 4 q (3 acoustic-Gamma + 6 genuine non-Gamma + 2 borderline). min = -77.14 cm-1 at q=(0,0,-0.800).
dominant_modes: stable el-ph dominated by the low-lying Ru-kagome optical modes at q3/q4 (e.g. dyn4 mode3 lambda=0.534 at 44.7 cm-1, dyn3 mode3 lambda=1.71 at 23.3 cm-1) and the high gamma E2g-like Si modes; imaginary modes sit in the 20-80 cm-1 kagome-tilt window.
lambda_flatband_question: PHYSICAL lambda=1.64 IS in the strong-coupling range (would-be high), consistent with a flat-band DOS boost (N(Ef)=35.4 states/spin/Ry is large). BUT the same flat-band-driven softness destabilizes the lattice at 2x2x2 -> cannot yet claim 'flat-band -> anomalously high lambda' as a clean result. Honest (c9/d6): the kagome flat band gives a LARGE el-ph drive that the coarse-grid lattice cannot support; the converged answer needs denser q.
qforge_xval: not run -- naive lambda.x assembly is corrupted by the instability so a QFORGE cross-val of the same (unstable) data adds no information; defer xval to a stable denser-q rerun.

Total lambda/omega_log/Tc therefore assembled DIRECTLY from the verbatim per-mode lambda(nu)+gamma+freq with q-star weights (the identical Allen-Dynes algorithm lambda.x implements). assembler = scripts/scratch (Python, /tmp/assemble_laru.py recipe embedded in CHANGELOG).
